questions.specification: Which of the following strategies is most effective for gathering user feedback on an early design concept during the early stages of user-centred design (UCD)?

options.markscheme: Surveys can support early user research, but they rely on self-reported opinions and are less effective for feedback on a specific design concept than observing users interacting with a prototype.

options.content: Analyzing market trends and sales data
options.markscheme: This is market research and does not directly gather user feedback on a design concept in a UCD process.

options.content: Performing user testing with low-fidelity prototypes
options.markscheme: Early low-fidelity prototype testing lets designers observe real user interaction, identify usability issues and pain points, and obtain actionable feedback to iterate the design—core to UCD.

options.content: Holding brainstorming sessions with the design team
options.markscheme: Brainstorming is an internal ideation method and does not collect feedback from users.

questions.specification: Which of the following strategies for user research is most effective for gathering in-depth qualitative data in User-Centered Design (UCD)?
Which of the following is a primary method used in User-Centred Design (UCD) to gather user feedback?

A small team is developing **HushBand**, a wearable for students and apprentices who work in noisy workshops and shared study spaces. The band has two modes:

- **Focus mode**: the band vibrates gently when the environment exceeds a user-set noise threshold.
- **Break mode**: the band suggests short breaks using haptic patterns and a subtle LED.

Early prototypes were built quickly using off-the-shelf components. After a week of informal trialling, the team received mixed feedback:

- Some users liked the vibration reminders but said they were **confusing** (they could not tell which pattern meant “noise too high” vs “take a break”).
- One user with sensory sensitivity found the vibration **uncomfortable**.
- Several users said they would only wear it if it looked like a normal fitness band.
- A workshop supervisor wanted evidence that it would not distract users doing precise tasks.

The team must now plan a user research programme to guide the next iteration, justify decisions to stakeholders, and reduce the risk of designing a product that works in theory but fails in real contexts.

Figure 1: Early HushBand prototype

![A photo of a simple wearable band prototype with a rectangular module taped onto a black elastic strap, showing a small LED and a button.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/1d97a266-8f0c-43d2-a6ef-c7237e8a14d2.png)

parts.content: Outline two reasons why the HushBand team should conduct user research **before** finalising the design.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies that user research reduces risk of designing the wrong solution by revealing real user needs, contexts, and constraints (e.g., workshop vs study space). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies that user research improves usability/acceptance by uncovering issues such as confusing feedback, comfort/sensory tolerance, or willingness to wear. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two principles the team should follow to make their user research findings **reliable and actionable**.
parts.markscheme:
- Use a representative sample (relevant user groups, abilities/sensitivities, contexts) to avoid biased conclusions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Triangulate methods (e.g., observation + interview + questionnaire) to validate findings from more than one source. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Use consistent tasks/protocols to improve comparability across participants/sessions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Avoid leading questions / minimise researcher bias to improve validity of responses. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two different user research methods the team could use to understand why some users find the vibration patterns confusing.
parts.markscheme:
- Moderated usability test with set tasks (e.g., interpret patterns, change threshold) while observing errors and time-on-task. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Semi-structured interview to explore interpretations, mental models, and preferences for feedback patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Contextual inquiry/field observation in the workshop/study space to see how cues are noticed during real activity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Questionnaire/survey to collect broader preferences and comprehension ratings for alternative patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The team is deciding between three feedback options for Focus mode: 

A. vibration only

B. LED only

C. vibration + LED. 

Explain how the team could use a **mixed-method** user research strategy to decide between the three.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe a quantitative component (e.g., A/B/C testing) measuring performance such as correct interpretation rate, reaction time, error rate, or distraction rating. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe a qualitative component (e.g., think-aloud, interview) to understand *why* users prefer/struggle with an option (comfort, attention, stigma, sensory sensitivity). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain sampling/context: testing with intended users in realistic environments (workshop/study) to capture contextual effects (gloves, lighting, noise, task load). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain how results are compared and synthesised (triangulation) to reach a decision rather than relying on a single metric or opinion. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link the strategy to decision-making: choosing the option that best balances clarity, comfort, and non-distraction for the target users. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain three specific pieces of design guidance that could be produced to improve the next HushBand iteration.
parts.markscheme:
- Guidance about feedback mapping: define distinct, learnable haptic patterns with clear meaning (e.g., one pattern for noise threshold, another for break suggestion) and document rules for pattern length/intensity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guidance about comfort/accessibility: include adjustable vibration intensity/off options and accommodate sensory-sensitive users; specify acceptable intensity ranges and default settings. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guidance about context-of-use: specify interaction requirements for workshops (e.g., single large button usable with gloves, visibility under low/high light) derived from observation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guidance about reducing distraction: set limits on frequency/duration of alerts during precision tasks; include a “do not disturb” or task mode informed by field testing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guidance about form factor/acceptance: specify aesthetic/fit requirements (looks like fitness band, strap sizes, materials) based on willingness-to-wear research. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guidance about language/communication: create concise onboarding instructions and iconography based on misconceptions discovered in testing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each guidance point is explained as an actionable, design-directing requirement tied to a user research insight (not a vague suggestion). <marks>3 marks</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following strategies for user research is most effective for obtaining in-depth qualitative data in User-Centered Design (UCD)?
